speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: What to cut out, or what to do different, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I never expected you to do that. That's four hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I would like to see it sometime.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you can take it if you promise to bring it back. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [LAUGHS] Then I'll go, oh, no!

speaker: interviewer
text: When did, when did [? Collin ?] do those other tapes? [INAUDIBLE] 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, you were telling me about when you were in Prague and deciding where you would go next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we left off there. Yes. Then my husband decided to smuggle some furs he had brought along and I had brought along, hidden in a suitcase and so on, to Holland, where he had friends from Germany in the fur business, to make that-- these furs into money. Because the only thing we got saved and knew where to go to next was because we had money. So it wasn't a fortune, but compared to other people, we had several hundred dollars-- American dollars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your money come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had smuggled out furs. And I had some, too, when I left, in the suitcase, on the baby, and as a blanket I took a piece of fur. Anyway, he smuggled that to Holland. And when the Nazis came into Prague, the, the Czechoslovaks didn't like it. And you could bribe them. So he bought himself a ticket to go on a train to Holland. He had to do that in order to ship the suitcases with the furs to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he never got on that train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after this, the furs, the furs were in Holland, so he had to go there to make it into money. So I didn't-- couldn't go from one country to another with a baby and smuggle myself. So he-- we found out that I could go legally to Italy for six months as a Czechoslovakian citizen. So-- How did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from other people. You know, there were a lot of people there. Or you could even go to the consulate there and ask them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the decision was that I have to go to Italy with the baby, and he will go back to Berlin, like I did, too. But we needed a visa. So that wasn't easy to get. The Germans asked me, why do you want to go back to Berlin? So I said, well, my mother is sick. She sent me a telegram. Which she had done. And we tried to get a visa, and we didn't get it. And-- because they ask also, are you Jewish? Yes. No, get out. So I never looked very Jewish, so-- they said my father-in-law and my husband didn't look very Jewish, but maybe I looked less Jewish. So I went with a child, though. We always thought maybe with a child, you know, it's better. Anyway, I was thrown out all over the place. So then, my father-in-law went with me to a Czechoslovakian place where they issued visas, and he spoke Czech to them. And they were, like I said, not very happy with the German invasion. And this man said to me, you are Catholic, aren't you? Who said that to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Czechoslovakian-- whatever he was-- officer. And I said yes. So I got my visa for 24 hours, go through Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel. So he didn't say anything. And he didn't say yes. [BACKROUND NOISE] I couldn't say it for him, and having the name [? Samuel, ?] so-- we figured that isn't, maybe, too good. So he didn't say anything. A week or two later, after I had left, he went back and the same man gave him a visa without even asking. Maybe he did ask him. My father-in-law didn't tell me. I wouldn't know. He came a week or two later, also through Berlin. And I went to Italy, just picked one place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you want to go from Prague to Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to go through Berlin to go to Italy. Because a lot of people fled from Czechoslovakia, especially the men, to Poland to save themselves. And my husband said, there's no use to go through Poland, and I'm not going alone anyway. I'm not going to leave you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people who went to Poland, they were either shot on the border, or they-- then they went on to Russia. So my husband said, it's no use. We have to go west and not east. What did you do in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just say hi to my parents and stayed one night and left. Couldn't stay any longer. And then my mother went with me on a train to the Italian border, where she had to leave. And she asked me to leave the baby with her, and she will bring it to me wherever I'm going to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, would you have left me? And she said, no. And I said, well, I'm not going to leave him either. So I left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Italy, you could stay legally six months. My father-in-law came a week or two later. And the two of us, with Henry, spent-- I don't know. It wasn't six months-- maybe three, four months there. In the meantime, my husband had smuggled himself from Germany to Belgium. From Belgium there was a streetcar-- and like I said, these people, you could bribe these people-- a streetcar conductor. He got on, and they took him into France. And in France, we had an uncle who went there in 1933, when he couldn't marry his non-Jewish girlfriend on account of the Nuremberg laws. And he was living there and had a six-year-old daughter, so my husband went to them after he had sold the furs in Holland, in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now he told me, you have to come to France and-- to be together again. And he found out that we could go to Shanghai, and they would pay for it. We didn't have that much money to pay the trip for the three of us to go to Shanghai. And on the phone, I said, what did you say? [LAUGHS] He said, Shanghai. Well, OK. How do I get into France? So he said, well, let me know. And I had American dollars. He sent me American dollars. No, first I went to a Jewish jewelry store in this little town of Alassio on the Riviera. And I told them my situation, and they said, well, if you have 30 American dollars, we help you smuggle yourself over to France. But it has to be American dollars.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he sent me the money. And then I went to a German lady in the next town with the baby. And she agreed, yes, $30. And the father-- my husband paid a ticket for his father to Shanghai. So he could go legally through friends to Marseilles and get on a boat. After he had seen that I will get out, he left. And then I came to this German lady, as planned, and she said, I didn't know you have a baby. You have to give me another $10. I said, you told me I can't have another penny with me, because when they catch us, me-- if I have no money on me, they let me go. But if I have money on me, they throw me in jail. And she said, yeah, but I didn't know you have a baby, and the baby will cry and give the-- gives us away. They'll put you on a little fishing boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would throw you in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: France. France or Italians. They wanted this American money. So I said, well, I don't have another penny. And she said, well, then give me the child, and I bring him on the train to Nice in France. And I said, no, I wouldn't do that. Well, I don't want your child. I said, well, I still don't-- I'd rather-- I don't know what, but I am not doing it. So anyway, then she took the little bit of stuff I had on me-- very little. I remember a photo-- a camera.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was always-- like pictures, I have them all here, yet, too. Camera, and a wool blanket I had for the baby, and little bit of clothes, including diapers and stuff like that. And she took all of that away from me. I said, what did you do that for? It's not even of value. I just want to be sure you send me the $10 when you're in France. I said, I promised you that. You're saving my life, so to say. How would I not keep that promise? So anyway, she took that all away. And then she went with me one night down to the-- no, what was it-- Mediterranean there. And between the cliffs, steep cliffs, there was a little boat waiting for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 refugees in there, and the people who ran the boat, Italians. And there were about nine men, myself, and Henry. And they helped me with holding him and keeping him, you know, carrying him, so that he was not crying and give, give us away. And then we landed somewhere, and we were told to go-- walk through the woods in a certain direction for one hour. And we would come to a place, to a French lady who was also involved, and she will help us get to Nice. So we walked. And a lot of people were taken that way, that all that was a lie. But she opened the door, and I saw her French [? calendar, ?] and I heard her French-- speaking French, and we were safe. We were in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, taxis came. They were all involved in that smuggling. And taxis came and took us to Nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the middle of the night, and I got a room with Henry. The others had to sleep in the lobby on the floor. And then, this was the first time in my life that I had to get-- go food stamps. I had not-- didn't have a penny on me, like she said. And there were Jewish organizations, like the HIAS or the Joint. They had different names at that time. And they gave me food stamps, so I could get a meal, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they interviewed me, said, what are your plans now? I said, I got to get to, to Paris. Where were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nice. And that's an-- was an 18-hour train, train ride. Yeah. All wet clothes. Baby was wet. I was wet from, you know, the waves coming over that little boat. So they said, how did you plan on getting there? I said, well, if you want to call my husband in Paris at this and this number, he will send the money for the train tickets. And he did. And then they said, don't you dare to open your mouth on that 18-hour train trip, because it would give you away. My language-- I even could speak a little French, French. But the accent and all that-- so I made it to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in France legally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they smuggled us into France. And, and from France, my husband had found out that if we-- if I-- I have to be in Paris to get on the boat to Shanghai, and to get the tickets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there. We went to the office there. It took three weeks. And we rode the train back down to Marseilles and got on the boat. How long were you in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you live with?

speaker: interviewee
text: The uncle-- uncle, aunt, and the little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Richard Sinasohn, S-I-N-A-S-O-H-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Margaret.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Renee. Yeah. And this little Renee, then six years old, she helped us. She talked for us. We went to a department store. I had to buy little clothes or what. She went around. We didn't open our mouths. She-- [LAUGHS] people thought we were deaf or dumb or whatever. And then we got on a train back down to the coast again and on the ship to Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that crossing. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were all kidding each other, we're going to America, you know. We didn't want to admit we were going to Shanghai. And I called my parents before I got on the boat, and my parents said, are you kidding?

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, no, I'm not kidding. The boat is right here, a big, big boat. Who goes to Shanghai? And I said, people who want to save themselves. Well, I don't know. And he wasn't so sure of himself then. Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father. That-- he said, let-- you rent a store, and I'm going to teach your husband the butcher business. We're going to have a butcher shop. And I said, yeah, it sounds good. But I knew it wouldn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got on the boat, and my sister got herself tickets in, in Berlin to get on the boat to Shanghai. I think they went to Italy to live. And they called my parents up from the office and said, we are here. We're buying our tickets. Because they didn't want to let us go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you come down here and you buy yourself two tickets. No, we're going to wait for America. We have papers, and we're going to wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll bring you kids over from out of Shanghai. Who wants to live in Shanghai? I said, we don't want to, either, but we want to get away. We have to get away. So that was the last time I talked to them. The papers weren't in order. The cousin, my cousin, their nephew-- hadn't put in his income tax return. So that's what the council wants to see, what he earns, so he can support these two people. There were different laws than they are nowadays. He was a sponsor, and he had to show that he can support those two people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't do that, and then doubled back, and it got too late. The war broke out, and that was it. The furniture, everything was packed, and, and whatever they had, people brought legally to America at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the trip from France to Shanghai take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three weeks. And, well, for us, like I always say, we were young, and it was a little bit of an adventure. I wondered why they cover everything up once we were out of Nice for a while, and I found out we were in Egypt. And it got so hot. We had never experienced stuff like that. We looked down. There was a desert and tents and people living in-- it-- I don't say we enjoyed it. But when you're 24 and 25 and 26 years old, things are a little different than when you're my, my parents in the 50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many other refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thousands of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: On that boat?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I had the, the ticket here in my papers. I can't remember right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, fine. No problem. Good food. There was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Plentiful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. It was a French boat. They gave everybody wine, and the babies, too. [LAUGHS] I said, my kid doesn't drink wine. But they filled it half or more, half full of water, and then a sip of wine. I guess French people still do that nowadays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive in Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if I know the date. I think it was July 7, 1939. We were exactly one year on the run, so to say, in Europe. And we arrived there in 115 degrees heat and a lot of mosquitoes. And I got on a rickshaw. That was the second time we stopped in Saigon. It has a different name now. It was Vietnam. And I saw the rickshaw there the first time, and we said, we're not going on a rickshaw and let human beings pull us. And they say, you have to do that. They make a living that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we got-- when I got in the rickshaw in Shanghai, well, I still-- I had that feeling, I think, all those years. It's, it's a shame that people have to do that for a living. But the truth was they wanted us for customers. So it was a Sabbath, where my father-in-law and my sister-in-law didn't ride. So they walked next to my rickshaw. Because I had the baby, I was allowed to take the rickshaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: My in-laws had rented us one room. And my mother-in-law was always very cheerful and encouraging. And she said, we're all here together, and we have nothing to complain. We hope that my parents and her youngest son, they will make it, plus all the rest of the people they want to come. But we have nothing to complain.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selma Sinasohn. She was born named Sinasohn, her maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same as the uncle in Paris, S-I-N-A-S-O-H-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your living quarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: One room. Nothing. No toilet, no kitchen, nothing. We washed clothes in cold water, hung it on the bamboo sticks, I think it's called, that goes from one window to another across a lane. The lane was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the lane had a lot of little houses, so to say. And downstairs, everybody washed in cold water. And like I say, we hung it on the bamboo sticks. And in the wintertime, it got so stiff that you couldn't even use it when it was dry.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to support yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't to begin with. We didn't later, either. We got help from-- well, the names were all different, the organizations. The Red Cross came in there, too, in the war, during the war. And we, we had to-- we had big camps where my husband and I, or my family-- the others also didn't go-- only the first night we had to go there to register, so to say, to be eligible for the food. And so then we all went every day with a little pot and picked up the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the camps. There was a big kitchen. The Kitchen Fund, I think they called it in Shanghai. And we picked out our food, which was, you know, lots of noodles. We ate lot of rice. When we were still hungry, we had one pound of rice for the three of us. And we had no electricity or anything. It was a little stove. You had to fan it, the briquettes. And no, no paper, no kindling. Very hard. My mother-in-law had asthma. She couldn't do it. So that was my job. She was really-- my father-in-law and her were the ones that encouraged us young ones. And they said, we work together. The family sticks together. We'll all make it. Don't worry. Times will be better again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once the war was over, of course, then came the big worry about my brother-in-law, who had gone to Holland, get ready to Israel with his-- with the orphanage where he worked in Frankfurt am Main as a teacher. And the whole orphanage was shipped to Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we had a ticket for him. But the director of this orphanage called my in-laws and said, you can't take him away from his children. He's the father of 30 or 50 children. They all love him, and he loves them. And he will go with them to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father-in-law said to my mother-in-law, how can you tell, tell somebody go to Shanghai when he has the opportunity to go to Israel? And, of course, it wasn't Israel then. It was Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she didn't really want to agree, so my husband also-- he said, really, no. We should be together, like you say. But they gave in. He pleaded. He wanted to stay with those children. And they got killed, too. He got picked up in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your brother-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother-in-law?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brother-in-law was Herbert, Herbert Haas. And he married a girl by the name of [? Eva ?] [? Zimmer ?] in, in the camp they were-- they were in Holland. And I met just the first time a few years ago a brother of hers in Denmark. And both were picked up. I think she was-- they were married just a month or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, through the Red Cross after the war. And then, just lately, I had a letter from the Red Cross-- it was in a paper here-- that there are new places in Germany where they'll still look for people. They get-- they find more and more papers. The Germans were always very organized and exact, and they find more and more papers about people. And they looked up my parents again, and there, I just found the date. They were picked up on, I think, March 1st, it said, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were picked up?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Herbert-- my parents. And Herbert was picked up from Holland. There are two different dates. I can't remember right now. But one of the two was picked up one day, and the other one three, four days later, and they never saw-- never saw each other again, as far as we know.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Shanghai, did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all we could do is go to the nightclubs where my husband played the music. He learned how to-- he was very musical. He had a good voice. And the people told him if he can play an instrument, they would hire him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he learned how to-- had one lesson, I guess, from another drummer. Bought himself an old drum and went every night, singing and drumming. And during the day, he worked at everything and anything he could lay his hand on.

speaker: interviewee
text: One time he stood on the street 4, 5 o'clock in the morning and sold bread or-- for us, for the refugees-- from a bakery. We had our own bakeries, our own butcher shops, our own restaurants. And-- but all that didn't give us a living. I went with him then, as a waitress and worked a little bit until we had to move into the-- what they called district. The Japanese walked in in 1943 and sent all of us, 18,000 or 20,000, into a certain part of Shanghai, where we already lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hongkew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hongkew.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the people-- a few people, not too many-- lived outside of this district. The people who had jobs or could make a living without help, not like us, they had to move back where we were. They had to move in. And then, what I found out now is that the Germans wanted the Japanese to do the same with us what they did in Germany, but the Japanese refused. All they did was put us in a district.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did life change for you once the Japanese occupied Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if we already had a little job, like my husband, and me, too, we had to go to the police and get a passport to leave the district. And there was a Japanese called [NON-ENGLISH] who, who said he's-- who called himself the King of the Jews. He was absolutely crazy. He just did what he wanted to do. Either he loved you or he hated you. He slapped a few people. He had to stand up on the table to reach some of the men, because he was also very short. And the-- some of those European men-- you know, not a lot, but-- were much taller than he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he slapped a few in the face. Or a girlfriend of mine, he took the makeup off. He, he said, you want to look like Japanese girl? You are no Japanese girl. And crazy things.

speaker: interviewee
text: We just found out that he loved children. And so when I went once and was there, and after standing in line for hours and hours, he said, no. No passport. And I said, I have baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Baby? Yeah. Yeah, baby, pass. Baby has to eat. Why were you-- why were you-- did you need a passport at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I left the district to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you going to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, either the international settlement, French concession, the rest of Shanghai. That Hongkew was a little bombed-out part of China, of Shanghai, where the Japanese and the Chinese had a war in 1936. And these houses we moved in, they were damaged from the war. But some people knew how to build it and correct it, and we lived in them. Not-- they weren't all bombed-out. But my in-laws, for instance, they had 100 American dollars. They used that, and they moved in there and had people repair it and lived in there for nine years we were there, which was a little better. They had a grocery store in the front. Upstairs, one and a half rooms. So they rented out that half a room, and they slept in the other. And we ate in the living-- in the store that-- not every day we ate with them, but Saturday, again, and Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to keep Shabbat in Hongkew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. She had trained a Chinese lady to turn on the lights. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-in-law. She said, they understand German. When I talk to them, [LAUGHS] they understand. Anyway, she trained a Chinese amah, as they're called, to help us there. And she, she tried. They tried, my in-laws. We didn't care. We ourselves didn't keep all that. But there was a synagogue where my brother-in-law was a cantor. And that was in walking distance, because they didn't ride on the Sabbath. And so we walked to this little temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a book here where it's in there, but I can't remember. It was a Russian synagogue. And it was very little, and a lot of us went there. But there they-- synagogues sprang up all around us. People who had one room, you know, they made room for, for people to come in and pray. Laymen, not all, all schooled people. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother-in-law, the cantor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was a cantor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] And he was a cantor there all those years. But he also didn't make enough. My sister-in-law had boarders, cooked for boarders. And so nobody even-- two people could work and didn't make a living. Besides, there was nothing there to cook with. And it, it-- all of us depended on the help.

speaker: interviewer
text: The help of who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the camps. The Kitchen Fund was the name there, whoever funded that. At different times, different people, organizations, funded it, especially when the war broke out. They didn't get the American money anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will pause now for a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
